LightGBM Agentic AI Credit Risk

On this page

  • Literature Review

Literature Review

Author

Supriya Sathe (Advisor: Dr. Cohen)

Literature Review

A major change in perspective is taking place in the field of credit risk management, by adding a dynamic Machine Learning (ML) frameworks that prioritize regulatory interpretability and prediction accuracy replacing static statistical scoring system.

In the past, logistic modeling and other basic linear models were used for evaluating risk from credit score or assessment . However, Bussmann et al. (2020)[1] suggested that modern artificial intelligence models greatly improved the ability to recognize complicated, dynamic trends in financial organizations. Bussmann et al. (2020)[1] highlighted that these models need to be explained so as to be actually used in handling risks. They demonstrate how model outcomes can be split down into specific feature components by using statistical techniques like SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), enabling management to validate the theories and at the same time derive the choices regarding credit. Shreya and Pathak (2025)[2] support this by emphasizing that XAI is the main link between the efficiency of ML and the integrity standards of modern banking institutions.

The use of XAI is a solution to worldwide regulations rather than this current technological solution. In their research on the use of XAI in banks, De Lange et al. (2022)[3] pointed out that it is technically challenging to utilize transparent modelling for credit rejections due to the “obligation to explanation” required by standards like the General Data Protection Regulation as per the the laws. According to their study, while collaborative methods like Gradient Boosting deliver high AUC ratings, they need specific explanatory tools (like Waterfall , Agile or Impact charts) trying to provide consumers and the banking industry the fine-grained insights they need for credit score processing

A diverse approach in the modern world that integrates quantitative data science with business goals and strategy is increasingly being used to analyze current consumer’s evaluations of credit. Oyeyemi et al. (2025)[4] support a comprehensive approach in which XAI is a fundamental part of organizational analytics instead of only a post-hoc analysis method. They claim that institutions may more focus accurately correlate their risk profile with economic circumstances by explaining the “why” underlying model results, turning a technical forecast into a useful business insight for the advancement.

According to current business viewpoints, autonomous technologies will replace the human interpretation in the next generation of credit risk and any other areas as well. The projected growth of corporate credit, according to McKinsey & Company (2025)[5], is “agentic,” in which AI agents control the entire credit procedure—from initial data collection to final decision-making after going through the process—all while enforcing an extensive level of accessibility for consumer’s trust.

To go further, Kubam (2024)[6] outlines an algorithm of “Agentic AI” that permits independent, quick, and understandable making of decisions. As a way to maintain manual human-oriented control over the machine even as it grows increasingly autonomous, these agents are built to track their own outcomes and provide simple analyses of their decision making. This project understands this dynamic horizon as the setting in which powerful, transparent models will ultimately work, even if it concentrates on using LightGBM as the primary estimation algorithm.

Based on the reviewed literature, I think there is still a critical need for frameworks that can handle the particular characteristics of banking information (such as disparities in classes and classification characteristics) without losing accessibility towards customers, even though the financial services sector is quickly trying to move to complicated, high-speed algorithms like LightGBM. As developing a LightGBM pipeline that makes use of Shapley values \((\phi_i)\) to offer a strong auditing trail for credit, this project covers that gap and assures that the algorithm is both “state-of-the-art” in terms efficiency and “state-of-the-compliant” in terms of accessibility.

References

1. Bussmann, N., Giudici, P., Marinelli, D., & Papenbrock, J. (2021). Explainable machine learning in credit risk management. Computational Economics, 57(1), 203–216.
2. Shreya, & Pathak, H. (2025). Explainable artificial intelligence credit risk assessment using machine learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.19383
3. De Lange, P. E., Melsom, B., Vennerød, C. B., & Westgaard, S. (2022). Explainable AI for credit assessment in banks. Journal of Risk and Financial Management, 15(12), 556.
4. Oyeyemi, D. O., Okosieme, O. O., Idowu-Kunlere, O., Julius, E. A., & Nwinyi, I. P. (n.d.). Explainable AI for credit risk assessment: Integrating machine learning with business analytics.
5. Nuzzo, A., Kremer, A., Tavakol, A., Harreis, H., Schneider, S., & Beitz, S. (2025). The future is agentic: AI’s role in the end-to-end corporate credit process. McKinsey. https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/risk-and-resilience/our-insights/the-future-is-agentic-ais-role-in-the-end-to-end-corporate-credit-process
6. Kubam, C. S. (2025). Agentic AI for autonomous, explainable, and real-time credit risk decision-making. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2601.00818.
